title: Share of new cancers caused by infections
subtitle: Estimated share of new [cancer](#dod:cancer) cases attributed to all known cancer-causing pathogens. This includes [H. pylori](#dod:helicobacter-pylori), [human papillomavirus](#dod:human-papillomavirus), [hepatitis B & C virus](#dod:hepatitis-virus), Epstein-Barr virus, human herpesvirus type 8, schistosoma haematobium, human T-cell lymphotropic virus, O. viverrini, and C. sinensis.
note: [Non-melanoma skin cancers](#dod:non-melanoma-skin-cancer) are excluded due to potentially incomplete records and inconsistent registry practices.
caption:
Share of new cancers caused by infections

Estimated share of new cancer cases attributed to all known cancer-causing pathogens. This includes H. pylori, human papillomavirus, hepatitis B & C virus, Epstein-Barr virus, human herpesvirus type 8, schistosoma haematobium, human T-cell lymphotropic virus, O. viverrini, and C. sinensis.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated share of new cancer cases attributed to known cancer-causing pathogens globally in 2020. The data is sorted by percentage, ranging from 100% down to 1.2%. At the top of the chart, four cancer types show a 100% attribution rate: Kaposi's sarcoma, cervical carcinoma, anus squamous cell carcinoma, and adult T-cell leukemia and lymphoma. Following these, non-cardia stomach cancer sits at 89%, while nasopharynx carcinoma and vagina carcinoma register 83.1% and 78% respectively. Hepatocellular carcinoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma in stomach appear in the high 70s range. A middle cluster includes Burkitt lymphoma at 60.1%, Hodgkin lymphoma and penis carcinoma both near 51%, and oropharyngeal carcinoma at 30.7%. Vulva carcinoma and cardia stomach cancer fall in the 20% to 25% range. The bar labeled "Cancers overall" indicates a global average of 13.1%. Below this average, several specific cancers show lower attribution shares: other non-Hodgkin lymphoma at 3.3%, bile duct cancer at 2.6%, laryngeal cancer at 2.2%, oral cancer at 2.1%, and bladder carcinoma at the bottom with 1.2%. The chart notes that non-melanoma skin cancers are excluded from these estimates due to potentially incomplete records and inconsistent registry practices.
